perturbation.original_text: 1.7 "Defect" shall mean any failure of Licensed Technology to meet the Specification as a result of a material failure (including an error or "bug" that is material) of the Licensed Technology. "Material" is defined as priority A, B, and C in Article 6.3.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.7 "Defect" shall mean any failure of Licensed Technology to meet the Specification. "Material" shall mean something is visible to the user.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Defect" refers to a "material failure" defined by priority levels in Article 6.3, providing a clear standard. The modified text removes this reference and defines "Material" subjectively as 'something is visible to the user'. This introduces ambiguity and contradicts the intention of the contract, since visibility does not necessarily mean a critical or high priority issue, which would likely have been required to be classified as a Defect. Therefore, the term Defect has become misaligned.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 395-a covers implied warranties in the sale of new homes. While this contract isn't about homes, the principle is relevant. It sets a standard that goods (or, by analogy, software) must be free from material defects. By making 'material' subjective and only about what's visible, the contract weakens the implied warranty by setting a much lower standard for what constitutes a 'defect' than what a court would likely consider 'material' under warranty law, as 'material' is generally interpreted to mean something that affects the functionality or value of the product.
perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts legal principles related to implied warranties. The original contract defined 'Defect' with reference to specific priority levels (A, B, C), setting a clear, objective standard. The changed text replaces this with a subjective definition based on user visibility. The LLM-provided law explanation references NY GBL § 395-a, noting that 'material' in warranty law typically means something affecting functionality or value. By defining 'material' as merely 'something is visible to the user', the modified contract lowers the standard for what constitutes a 'defect', potentially undermining implied warranty protections. The provided snippet from NYSenate.gov pertains to an unrelated section of the GBL (disclosure of prior use) and not specifically to warranties or the definition of 'material defects'. Nevertheless, the general principle that material defects affect implied warranties applies to software, making the change a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 1.5 "Update" shall mean a new release of a software product which typically includes bug fixes and/or minor feature changes, but does not include substantial new functionality.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.5 "Update" shall mean any alteration to the Licensed Technology including, but not limited to, bug fixes, minor feature changes, and/or substantial new functionality.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Update' excludes 'substantial new functionality.' The modified definition explicitly includes it. This change broadens the term 'Update' to encompass significant changes, potentially impacting the licensee's expectations and the pricing structure associated with updates versus new versions.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-209 concerns modification of contracts. If 'Update' now includes substantial new functionality, it blurs the line between an update (covered under maintenance) and a new version (potentially requiring a new license fee). This could be viewed as an attempt to modify the contract without proper consideration or agreement on pricing for the new functionality, potentially violating the UCC's good faith requirement for contract modifications. The modification introduces uncertainty, as it's unclear if providing substantial new functionality as an 'update' waives the right to charge for it as a 'new version'.

perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly excludes 'substantial new functionality' from the definition of 'Update,' while the changed text explicitly includes it. This creates a misalignment and contradiction because it alters the scope of what is considered an 'Update.' According to the Law Explanation, UCC § 2-209 addresses contract modifications, and including substantial new functionality could be interpreted as an attempt to modify the contract without proper consideration or agreement on pricing, potentially violating the UCC's good faith requirement. The modified definition introduces uncertainty, potentially impacting pricing and creating ambiguity regarding the licensee's obligations. The change falls under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the contractual language deviates from the original legally recognized definition within the contract, potentially making it unenforceable as it relates to the original intention of updates vs. new versions, which could affect pricing and scope.

perturbation.original_text: 11. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR LOST PROFITS OR ANY CONSEQUENTIAL, SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL, OR INDIRECT DAMAGES OR SUCH OTHER PARTY, HOWEVER CAUSED AND ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY, ARISING OUT OF THIS AGREEMENT. THESE LIMITATIONS SHALL APPLY NOTWITHSTANDING ANY FAILURE OF ESSENTIAL PURPOSE OF ANY LIMITED REMEDY. IN NO EVENT SHALL D2'S LIABILITY HEREUNDER EXCEED THE TOTAL AMOUNT PAID OR OWED BY LICENSEE TO D2 UNDER THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. LIMITATION OF LIABILITY IN NO EVENT SHALL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER PARTY FOR LOST PROFITS. IN NO EVENT SHALL D2'S LIABILITY HEREUNDER EXCEED THE TOTAL AMOUNT PAID OR OWED BY LICENSEE TO D2 UNDER THIS AGREEMENT.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause excludes liability for 'consequential, special, incidental, or indirect damages'. The modified clause only excludes liability for 'lost profits,' removing the broader protection against various types of damages. This makes the limitation of liability significantly narrower and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 2-719 allows for contractual limitations or exclusion of consequential damages unless the limitation is unconscionable. By removing the exclusion of 'consequential, special, incidental, or indirect damages,' the modified clause may be interpreted as a failure to adequately limit liability, especially concerning damages that are reasonably foreseeable. A court might find that limiting only lost profits without addressing other reasonably foreseeable damages renders the limitation of liability clause unconscionable and therefore unenforceable, especially if the remedy provided fails of its essential purpose. The enforceability now hinges on whether excluding only 'lost profits' provides a fair quantum of remedy in light of the breach.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 7 Remedies previous SECTION 2-718 Liquidation or Limitation of Damages; Deposits up PART 7 Remedies next SECTION 2-720 Effect of "Cancellation" or "Rescission" on Claims for Antecedent Breach This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-719 Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 7 Section 2--719. Contractual Modification or Limitation of Remedy. (1) Subject to the provisions of subsections (2) and (3) of this section and of the preceding section on liquidation and limitation of damages, (a) the agreement may provide for remedies in addition to or in substitution for those provided in this Article and may limit or alter the measure of damages recoverable under this Article, as by limiting the buyer's remedies to return of the goods and repayment of the price or to repair and replacement of non-conforming goods or parts; and (b) resort to a remedy as provided is optional unless the remedy is expressly agreed to be exclusive, in which case it is the sole remedy. (2) Where circumstances cause an exclusive or limited remedy to fail of its essential purpose, remedy may be had as provided in this Act. (3) Consequential damages may be limited or excluded unless the limitation or exclusion is unconscionable. Limitation of consequential damages for injury to the person in the case of consumer goods is prima facie unconscionable but limitation of damages where the loss is commercial is not.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the exclusion of 'consequential, special, incidental, or indirect damages,' which the original text explicitly included in its limitation of liability clause. NY UCC § 2-719(3), as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, allows for the limitation or exclusion of consequential damages unless it is unconscionable. By narrowing the exclusion to only 'lost profits,' the modified clause creates a contradiction because it potentially fails to adequately limit liability concerning reasonably foreseeable damages beyond lost profits. This could lead a court to deem the limitation of liability clause unconscionable and unenforceable. The modified text weakens the original protection by not addressing other types of damages, thus contradicting the intent of UCC § 2-719.
